title: Tuberculosis — Latent
oneLiner: Asymptomatic infection with *M. tuberculosis*, no active disease; treatment reduces future reactivation risk (especially in immunosuppressed, close contacts, children).

sections.Tuberculosis – Latent: **One-liner:** Asymptomatic infection with *M. tuberculosis*, no active disease; treatment reduces future reactivation risk (especially in immunosuppressed, close contacts, children).
sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Progression to active TB before diagnosis:** patient may have subclinical active disease → key finding: *any unexplained cough >3 weeks, night sweats, weight loss, fever, or abnormal CXR (even subtle apical shadowing, hilar lymphadenopathy)*. Never treat latent without excluding active TB.
- **Acute drug-induced liver injury (DILI):** especially from isoniazid + rifampicin regimen → key finding: *new jaundice, dark urine, pale stools, right upper quadrant pain, or vomiting within first 2 weeks of treatment*. Check LFTs at baseline and monthly; if ALT >5x ULN (or >3x with symptoms) → stop all drugs, call senior.
- **HIV co-infection with unmasking IRIS:** initiation of LTBI treatment in undiagnosed HIV can cause paradoxical worsening if ART started simultaneously → key finding: *new fever, lymphadenopathy, or cytopenia after starting treatment in at-risk patient*. Always check HIV serology before starting.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:** None (by definition) → patient is completely asymptomatic, normal exam.
- **Key history:** Recent close contact with smear-positive TB, prior incomplete treatment, travel from endemic area, immunosuppressive therapy (anti-TNF, steroids, post-transplant), HIV, or incarceration/homelessness.
- **Exam findings:** Normal vital signs, clear chest, no lymphadenopathy, no clubbing.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Active pulmonary TB** → *symptomatic* (cough, weight loss, fevers, night sweats), abnormal CXR, positive sputum AFB microscopy/Xpert MTB/RIF.
- **Sarcoidosis** → bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, often with skin/joint involvement, negative IGRA/Mantoux.
- **Fungal infection (histoplasmosis, coccidioidomycosis)** → travel history (Ohio River valley, Southwest US), often with eosinophilia, positive serology.
- **Atypical mycobacterial infection (e.g., *M. avium* complex)** → positive IGRA but negative Mantoux, often in immunocompromised, different treatment (macrolides).
- **Post-vaccination Mantoux positivity** → prior BCG can cause false-positive skin test (but IGRA is specific); no exposure history.

**Splitter:** CXR – normal (or only calcified Ghon focus/pleural thickening) is consistent with LTBI; *any parenchymal infiltrate, cavity, or pleural effusion* must be treated as active TB until proven otherwise.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** Symptom screen (cough, fever, night sweats, weight loss) – must be negative. Measure weight, pregnancy test in women of childbearing age (isoniazid/rifampicin teratogenicity – specialist discussion needed).
- **Bloods:** LFTs (baseline ALT, ALP, bilirubin), hepatitis B/C serology (in high-risk), HIV serology (mandatory per NICE; opt-out). FBC, renal function.
- **Imaging:** Chest X-ray (PA + lateral views) – if any abnormality, treat as active until sputum/exhaustive workup performed.
- **Special tests:**
  - **IGRA (interferon gamma release assay – T-SPOT.TB or QuantiFERON-Gold):** first-line (less affected by prior BCG).
  - **Mantoux (tuberculin skin test):** alternative; positive if ≥5mm induration (HIV+) or ≥10mm (others).
  - Both can be used; if positive and CXR normal/symptom-free → LTBI diagnosed.
- **Diagnostic criteria:** Positive IGRA or Mantoux + absent symptoms + normal CXR + no prior adequate treatment.
- **Never-miss test:** **Chest X-ray** – omission means you may treat a patient with active TB, risking spread and treatment failure. Also **HIV test** – untreated HIV with LTBI has high reactivation risk.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED / clinic):**
  - Rule out active TB → if symptom screen + CXR normal, proceed to offer chemoprophylaxis.
  - Check baseline LFTs, HIV status; arrange follow-up with TB specialist nurse or clinic.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Usually not admitted for LTBI alone.
  - If admitted for another reason and LTBI diagnosed, no isolation needed (not infectious). Ensure clear plan for outpatient treatment initiation.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **First-line regimen:** Rifampicin + Isoniazid for 3 months (both daily, fixed‑dose combination Rifater® not licensed for this; use separate tablets).
  - **Alternative (if rifampicin contraindicated):** Isoniazid alone for 6 months (+ pyridoxine).
  - **Alternative (if isoniazid resistant / intolerance):** Rifampicin alone for 4 months.
  - Ensure **pyridoxine 10–25 mg daily** co-prescribed with isoniazid.
  - Monitor LFTs monthly (week 0, 4, 8, 12 for 3‑month regimen; monthly for 6‑month regimen).
  - Provide written information about drug interactions (rifampicin: OCP, warfarin, antiretrovirals, methadone) and hepatotoxicity warning.
- **Default Orders (if admitted for other reason):** Not relevant for LTBI.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
| Drug | Dose | Route | Duration | Notes |
|------|------|-------|----------|-------|
| **Rifampicin** | 10 mg/kg (max 600 mg) | Oral once daily | 3 months | Always given with isoniazid; CYP450 inducer |
| **Isoniazid** | 5 mg/kg (max 300 mg) | Oral once daily | 3–6 months | Co‑prescribe pyridoxine 10–25 mg daily |
| **Pyridoxine (vitamin B6)** | 10–25 mg daily | Oral once daily | Duration of isoniazid | Prevents peripheral neuropathy |
| **Rifampicin alone** | 10 mg/kg (max 600 mg) | Oral once daily | 4 months | Alternative if isoniazid resistance/intolerance |
| **Isoniazid alone** | 5 mg/kg (max 300 mg) | Oral once daily | 6 months | Alternative if rifampicin contraindicated |

- **Contraindications/interactions:** Rifampicin → avoid in active liver disease, caution with alcohol; major inducer (warfarin, OCP, antiepileptics, ART – adjust doses). Isoniazid → caution if pre‑existing neuropathy, severe renal impairment.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Rifampicin – no dose adjustment in renal impairment. Isoniazid – consider dose reduction or extended interval in ESRD (e.g., 200 mg three times weekly – **[check local policy]**). Both – avoid if baseline ALT >3x ULN or active hepatitis.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Starting rifampicin + isoniazid **without checking baseline LFTs and ruling out active TB**. Second most – co‑prescribing with **warfarin** without INR monitoring and dose adjustment (warfarin dose may need doubling). Third – missing **pyridoxine** → severe peripheral neuropathy.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Hepatotoxicity (ALT >5x ULN or >3x with symptoms) – stop drugs, check hepatitis screen, refer to hepatology.
  - Hypersensitivity (fever, rash, eosinophilia) – stop both drugs, rechallenge cautiously under specialist advice.
  - Peripheral neuropathy (isoniazid) – prevented by pyridoxine; if occurs, stop isoniazid and treat with high‑dose pyridoxine.
  - Drug fever (rifampicin).
- **Chronic:**
  - Treatment failure (rare if LTBI, but risk of selection of drug resistance if active disease was missed).
  - Adherence failure – higher with 6‑month isoniazid monotherapy; use 3‑month combination to improve completion.
sections.Prognosis:
- Treatment reduces risk of reactivation by **60–70%** (isoniazid alone for 6 months) to **80–90%** (rifampicin + isoniazid for 3 months).
- Without treatment, lifetime risk of reactivation is ~10% (higher in HIV/immunosuppression). Mortality from reactivated TB is ~40–50% if untreated multi‑drug resistant; treatable disease.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always perform a chest X-ray and symptom screen before starting LTBI treatment** – this single step prevents inappropriate treatment of active TB.
- **Prescribe pyridoxine automatically** with any isoniazid-containing regimen – document the indication clearly.
- **Check HIV serology in every patient** – untreated HIV dramatically increases reactivation risk; LTBI treatment prevents it.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not treat LTBI without excluding active TB** – especially in symptomatic patients or those with abnormal CXR. This can lead to drug resistance and infectious spread.
- **Do not forget drug interactions** – rifampicin nullifies oral contraceptives, warfarin, and many antiretrovirals. Always discuss contraception and INR monitoring.
- **Do not start therapy if baseline ALT >3x ULN** – investigate cause first. Also avoid in patients with heavy alcohol use until liver function stabilises.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Jaundice, dark urine, pale stools, unexplained vomiting, or right upper quadrant pain (possible hepatitis).
  - New cough lasting >3 weeks, night sweats, weight loss, haemoptysis (possible active TB despite treatment).
  - Numbness, tingling in hands or feet (peripheral neuropathy – stop isoniazid, start pyridoxine 50 mg daily).
- **Follow-up:**
  - TB clinic or specialist nurse within 2 weeks of starting treatment for LFTs and adherence check.
  - Monthly LFTs and clinical review for duration of treatment.
  - Post‑treatment: no routine follow-up unless high risk (HIV, ongoing immunosuppression).
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (TB consultant/SpR) if:**
  - LFTs >5x ULN (or >3x ULN with jaundice/symptoms) – stop drugs, arrange urgent hepatology review.
  - Patient develops new symptoms suggestive of active TB (fever, cough, haemoptysis) – admit for airborne isolation, sputum cultures.
  - Anaphylaxis/hypersensitivity (widespread rash, fever, hypotension) – stop drugs, manage with antihistamines/steroids, rechallenge only under expert guidance.
  - Pregnant patient started on treatment – requires urgent specialist MDT (risk of hepatotoxicity vs reactivation).
- **NEWS2 >5 or lactate >2 mmol/L** – rare in LTBI alone, but if present, consider missed active TB (miliary/disseminated).

rawMarkdown:
## Tuberculosis – Latent

**One-liner:** Asymptomatic infection with *M. tuberculosis*, no active disease; treatment reduces future reactivation risk (especially in immunosuppressed, close contacts, children).

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?

- **Progression to active TB before diagnosis:** patient may have subclinical active disease → key finding: *any unexplained cough >3 weeks, night sweats, weight loss, fever, or abnormal CXR (even subtle apical shadowing, hilar lymphadenopathy)*. Never treat latent without excluding active TB.
- **Acute drug-induced liver injury (DILI):** especially from isoniazid + rifampicin regimen → key finding: *new jaundice, dark urine, pale stools, right upper quadrant pain, or vomiting within first 2 weeks of treatment*. Check LFTs at baseline and monthly; if ALT >5x ULN (or >3x with symptoms) → stop all drugs, call senior.
- **HIV co-infection with unmasking IRIS:** initiation of LTBI treatment in undiagnosed HIV can cause paradoxical worsening if ART started simultaneously → key finding: *new fever, lymphadenopathy, or cytopenia after starting treatment in at-risk patient*. Always check HIV serology before starting.

### Presentation

- **Cardinal symptoms:** None (by definition) → patient is completely asymptomatic, normal exam.
- **Key history:** Recent close contact with smear-positive TB, prior incomplete treatment, travel from endemic area, immunosuppressive therapy (anti-TNF, steroids, post-transplant), HIV, or incarceration/homelessness.
- **Exam findings:** Normal vital signs, clear chest, no lymphadenopathy, no clubbing.

### Differential Diagnosis

- **Active pulmonary TB** → *symptomatic* (cough, weight loss, fevers, night sweats), abnormal CXR, positive sputum AFB microscopy/Xpert MTB/RIF.
- **Sarcoidosis** → bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, often with skin/joint involvement, negative IGRA/Mantoux.
- **Fungal infection (histoplasmosis, coccidioidomycosis)** → travel history (Ohio River valley, Southwest US), often with eosinophilia, positive serology.
- **Atypical mycobacterial infection (e.g., *M. avium* complex)** → positive IGRA but negative Mantoux, often in immunocompromised, different treatment (macrolides).
- **Post-vaccination Mantoux positivity** → prior BCG can cause false-positive skin test (but IGRA is specific); no exposure history.

**Splitter:** CXR – normal (or only calcified Ghon focus/pleural thickening) is consistent with LTBI; *any parenchymal infiltrate, cavity, or pleural effusion* must be treated as active TB until proven otherwise.

### Investigations

- **Bedside:** Symptom screen (cough, fever, night sweats, weight loss) – must be negative. Measure weight, pregnancy test in women of childbearing age (isoniazid/rifampicin teratogenicity – specialist discussion needed).
- **Bloods:** LFTs (baseline ALT, ALP, bilirubin), hepatitis B/C serology (in high-risk), HIV serology (mandatory per NICE; opt-out). FBC, renal function.
- **Imaging:** Chest X-ray (PA + lateral views) – if any abnormality, treat as active until sputum/exhaustive workup performed.
- **Special tests:**
  - **IGRA (interferon gamma release assay – T-SPOT.TB or QuantiFERON-Gold):** first-line (less affected by prior BCG).
  - **Mantoux (tuberculin skin test):** alternative; positive if ≥5mm induration (HIV+) or ≥10mm (others).
  - Both can be used; if positive and CXR normal/symptom-free → LTBI diagnosed.
- **Diagnostic criteria:** Positive IGRA or Mantoux + absent symptoms + normal CXR + no prior adequate treatment.
- **Never-miss test:** **Chest X-ray** – omission means you may treat a patient with active TB, risking spread and treatment failure. Also **HIV test** – untreated HIV with LTBI has high reactivation risk.

### Management

- **Immediate (first hour / ED / clinic):**
  - Rule out active TB → if symptom screen + CXR normal, proceed to offer chemoprophylaxis.
  - Check baseline LFTs, HIV status; arrange follow-up with TB specialist nurse or clinic.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Usually not admitted for LTBI alone.
  - If admitted for another reason and LTBI diagnosed, no isolation needed (not infectious). Ensure clear plan for outpatient treatment initiation.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **First-line regimen:** Rifampicin + Isoniazid for 3 months (both daily, fixed‑dose combination Rifater® not licensed for this; use separate tablets).
  - **Alternative (if rifampicin contraindicated):** Isoniazid alone for 6 months (+ pyridoxine).
  - **Alternative (if isoniazid resistant / intolerance):** Rifampicin alone for 4 months.
  - Ensure **pyridoxine 10–25 mg daily** co-prescribed with isoniazid.
  - Monitor LFTs monthly (week 0, 4, 8, 12 for 3‑month regimen; monthly for 6‑month regimen).
  - Provide written information about drug interactions (rifampicin: OCP, warfarin, antiretrovirals, methadone) and hepatotoxicity warning.
- **Default Orders (if admitted for other reason):** Not relevant for LTBI.

### Drugs & Doses

| Drug | Dose | Route | Duration | Notes |
|------|------|-------|----------|-------|
| **Rifampicin** | 10 mg/kg (max 600 mg) | Oral once daily | 3 months | Always given with isoniazid; CYP450 inducer |
| **Isoniazid** | 5 mg/kg (max 300 mg) | Oral once daily | 3–6 months | Co‑prescribe pyridoxine 10–25 mg daily |
| **Pyridoxine (vitamin B6)** | 10–25 mg daily | Oral once daily | Duration of isoniazid | Prevents peripheral neuropathy |
| **Rifampicin alone** | 10 mg/kg (max 600 mg) | Oral once daily | 4 months | Alternative if isoniazid resistance/intolerance |
| **Isoniazid alone** | 5 mg/kg (max 300 mg) | Oral once daily | 6 months | Alternative if rifampicin contraindicated |

- **Contraindications/interactions:** Rifampicin → avoid in active liver disease, caution with alcohol; major inducer (warfarin, OCP, antiepileptics, ART – adjust doses). Isoniazid → caution if pre‑existing neuropathy, severe renal impairment.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Rifampicin – no dose adjustment in renal impairment. Isoniazid – consider dose reduction or extended interval in ESRD (e.g., 200 mg three times weekly – **[check local policy]**). Both – avoid if baseline ALT >3x ULN or active hepatitis.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Starting rifampicin + isoniazid **without checking baseline LFTs and ruling out active TB**. Second most – co‑prescribing with **warfarin** without INR monitoring and dose adjustment (warfarin dose may need doubling). Third – missing **pyridoxine** → severe peripheral neuropathy.

### Complications

- **Acute:**
  - Hepatotoxicity (ALT >5x ULN or >3x with symptoms) – stop drugs, check hepatitis screen, refer to hepatology.
  - Hypersensitivity (fever, rash, eosinophilia) – stop both drugs, rechallenge cautiously under specialist advice.
  - Peripheral neuropathy (isoniazid) – prevented by pyridoxine; if occurs, stop isoniazid and treat with high‑dose pyridoxine.
  - Drug fever (rifampicin).
- **Chronic:**
  - Treatment failure (rare if LTBI, but risk of selection of drug resistance if active disease was missed).
  - Adherence failure – higher with 6‑month isoniazid monotherapy; use 3‑month combination to improve completion.

### Prognosis

- Treatment reduces risk of reactivation by **60–70%** (isoniazid alone for 6 months) to **80–90%** (rifampicin + isoniazid for 3 months).
- Without treatment, lifetime risk of reactivation is ~10% (higher in HIV/immunosuppression). Mortality from reactivated TB is ~40–50% if untreated multi‑drug resistant; treatable disease.

### Pearls (positive actions)

- **Always perform a chest X-ray and symptom screen before starting LTBI treatment** – this single step prevents inappropriate treatment of active TB.
- **Prescribe pyridoxine automatically** with any isoniazid-containing regimen – document the indication clearly.
- **Check HIV serology in every patient** – untreated HIV dramatically increases reactivation risk; LTBI treatment prevents it.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)

- **Do not treat LTBI without excluding active TB** – especially in symptomatic patients or those with abnormal CXR. This can lead to drug resistance and infectious spread.
- **Do not forget drug interactions** – rifampicin nullifies oral contraceptives, warfarin, and many antiretrovirals. Always discuss contraception and INR monitoring.
- **Do not start therapy if baseline ALT >3x ULN** – investigate cause first. Also avoid in patients with heavy alcohol use until liver function stabilises.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)

- **Return if:**
  - Jaundice, dark urine, pale stools, unexplained vomiting, or right upper quadrant pain (possible hepatitis).
  - New cough lasting >3 weeks, night sweats, weight loss, haemoptysis (possible active TB despite treatment).
  - Numbness, tingling in hands or feet (peripheral neuropathy – stop isoniazid, start pyridoxine 50 mg daily).
- **Follow-up:**
  - TB clinic or specialist nurse within 2 weeks of starting treatment for LFTs and adherence check.
  - Monthly LFTs and clinical review for duration of treatment.
  - Post‑treatment: no routine follow-up unless high risk (HIV, ongoing immunosuppression).

### Escalation Criteria

- **Call senior (TB consultant/SpR) if:**
  - LFTs >5x ULN (or >3x ULN with jaundice/symptoms) – stop drugs, arrange urgent hepatology review.
  - Patient develops new symptoms suggestive of active TB (fever, cough, haemoptysis) – admit for airborne isolation, sputum cultures.
  - Anaphylaxis/hypersensitivity (widespread rash, fever, hypotension) – stop drugs, manage with antihistamines/steroids, rechallenge only under expert guidance.
  - Pregnant patient started on treatment – requires urgent specialist MDT (risk of hepatotoxicity vs reactivation).
- **NEWS2 >5 or lactate >2 mmol/L** – rare in LTBI alone, but if present, consider missed active TB (miliary/disseminated).
